about.description: An HTTP / HTTPS capture proxy tool with HTTPS MITM decryption, WebSocket, rewrite rules, breakpoints, and a plugin system.

global.requestHint: Pause all traffic before the request is sent, so you can edit the request headers / body and resume manually.
global.responseHint: Pause all traffic before the response is returned to the client, where you can edit the status code and response content.
global.title: Global breakpoints

paused.emptyHint: Once you enable the breakpoints above, matched requests will wait here
paused.title: Paused requests

rules.emptyHint: Add a URL matching rule (supports the * wildcard); matched requests / responses will be intercepted at the selected phase.
rules.title: Breakpoint rules

breakpoints.subtitle: Intercept requests / responses, edit them, then resume manually
breakpoints.title: Breakpoints

decrypt.notice: After the root certificate is installed and trusted, Sniffy dynamically issues certificates for the HTTPS sites you visit to enable MITM decryption. Some apps use certificate pinning and may not be decryptable even when the root certificate is trusted; this is an application-level security measure. Only debug on devices and traffic you are legally authorized to inspect.
decrypt.title: Decryption Notes
decrypt.whitelistHint: Configure the decryption scope under "Settings → HTTPS Decryption → Scope" (takes effect once the backend is wired up)

certs.noBackendTip: Not connected to backend; no certificate available
certs.regenerateConfirm: Regenerating the root certificate will immediately invalidate the old one. Every client that installed the old certificate must reinstall the new one. Continue?
certs.regenerateTip: Regenerate root CA (old certificate will be invalidated)

status.fingerprintHint: Verify the fingerprint matches before installing (read from the actual root certificate)
status.fingerprintPlaceholder: Shown after connecting to the backend
status.manageHint: Regenerating invalidates the old certificate; clients must reinstall
status.stateHint: Used to dynamically issue site certificates for decrypting HTTPS traffic
status.title: Root Certificate Status

android.1: Transfer the downloaded .crt certificate file to your phone's storage.
android.2: Open "Settings - Security - Encryption & credentials - Install a certificate from storage".
android.3: Select the certificate file, specify the usage as "CA certificate", and follow the prompts to finish.
android.4: Note: On Android 7+, apps do not trust user CAs by default; debugging requires a debuggable app.

ios.1: Point your iPhone's Wi-Fi proxy to this machine (host = the computer's LAN IP, port 8080).
ios.2: Scan the QR code on the right with Safari, or enter {{url}} directly.
ios.3: Allow the configuration profile to download, then go to "Settings - General - VPN & Device Management" and tap Install.
ios.4: After installing, go to "Settings - General - About - Certificate Trust Settings" and enable full trust for Sniffy Root CA.

macos.1: Download the certificate and double-click to open it; the system imports it into Keychain Access automatically.
macos.2: Find the Sniffy Root CA certificate in the "login" or "System" keychain and double-click it.
macos.3: Expand the "Trust" section and set "When using this certificate" to "Always Trust".
macos.4: Close the window and enter your system password to confirm the change.

windows.1: Click "Download Certificate (.crt)" above to save the root certificate file locally.
windows.2: Double-click the downloaded .crt file and click "Install Certificate" in the dialog.
windows.3: Choose "Local Machine" as the store location and place the certificate in the "Trusted Root Certification Authorities" store.
windows.4: Click "Finish" and restart your browser to decrypt HTTPS traffic.

certs.subtitle: Root certificate management and installation guide
certs.title: Certificates
